name: Lake Erie (Pennsylvania)
description:
Pennsylvania's 63-mile stretch of Lake Erie shoreline is one of the most productive and diverse fishing destinations in the Northeast. The lake's walleye fishery is world-renowned, with spring and fall runs producing fish in the 20- to 28-inch range along the shallow reefs and rocky structure. Steelhead Alley, the network of tributary streams flowing into the lake, draws fly anglers and spin fishermen from across the country each fall and winter for chrome-bright steelhead trout that average 6 to 10 pounds.

Preque Isle Bay and the Erie harbor area provide excellent boat launch facilities, while the open lake offers vast expanses of fishable water accessible from several public ramps along the shoreline. Smallmouth bass fishing is exceptional over the rocky reefs and gravel bars during summer, and yellow perch are a beloved target for families and panfish enthusiasts. The Pennsylvania Fish and Boat Commission maintains an aggressive stocking program for steelhead and walleye that keeps this Great Lake fishery among the best in the nation.

name: Raystown Lake
description:
Raystown Lake is the largest lake located entirely within Pennsylvania, stretching 28 miles through the Appalachian Mountains of Huntingdon County. The reservoir is best known for its trophy striped bass fishery, with landlocked stripers regularly exceeding 20 pounds and fish over 40 pounds caught each year. Largemouth and smallmouth bass both thrive in the lake's diverse habitat, from weedy coves and docks to rocky bluffs and deep ledges.

The Army Corps of Engineers operates multiple well-maintained boat ramps around Raystown Lake's 118-mile shoreline, and Seven Points Marina provides full-service facilities including boat rentals, fuel, and supplies. The lake's deep, clear water and dramatic cliff-lined shoreline create a stunning setting for fishing, and over 70 percent of the shoreline remains undeveloped forest. Walleye fishing has been steadily improving through stocking efforts, and crappie provide excellent spring panfishing in the sheltered coves and creek arms.

name: Pymatuning Lake
description:
Pymatuning Lake straddles the Pennsylvania-Ohio border and is one of the largest lakes in the region at 14,650 acres. The lake is famous among panfish enthusiasts for its outstanding crappie and perch fishing, with crappie in the 10- to 14-inch range caught regularly around submerged timber and brush piles. Walleye are the premier predator species, and the Pennsylvania Fish and Boat Commission maintains an active stocking program that produces consistent catches throughout the year.

Pymatuning State Park on the Pennsylvania side provides several boat ramps and extensive shoreline access, along with camping, cabins, and picnic facilities. The lake's relatively shallow depth and fertile waters support explosive fish growth, and musky have become an increasingly popular target with fish over 40 inches caught each season. Linesville is famous for its spillway where visitors feed bread to massive carp, creating the spectacle of ducks walking on the backs of fish. The lake's accessibility and consistent fishing make it one of northwestern Pennsylvania's most popular family fishing destinations.

name: Lake Wallenpaupack
description:
Lake Wallenpaupack is a 5,700-acre reservoir in the heart of Pennsylvania's Pocono Mountains, offering outstanding multi-species fishing just a short drive from the New York and Philadelphia metro areas. The lake is one of the top walleye fisheries in northeastern Pennsylvania, with spring and fall providing the best catches along rocky points and gravel shoals. Smallmouth bass thrive in the clear, rocky sections of the lake, while largemouth bass hold in the weedier bays and around docks.

Several public boat ramps provide access to Lake Wallenpaupack's 52 miles of scenic shoreline, and the lake's full-service marinas offer boat rentals, fuel, and guided fishing trips. Striped bass have been stocked in the reservoir and provide exciting open-water fishing for anglers trolling large lures and live bait. Musky add a trophy element to the fishery, and yellow perch are popular with families and ice anglers during winter months. The Pocono Mountains resort community surrounding the lake ensures plenty of dining, lodging, and recreation options for fishing vacations.

name: Delaware River
description:
The Delaware River forms Pennsylvania's eastern border, flowing over 200 miles from the upper reaches near Hancock, New York, to the tidal waters below Trenton. The upper and middle Delaware is one of the finest smallmouth bass rivers in the Eastern United States, with bronzebacks averaging 12 to 16 inches found in the riffles, eddies, and deep pools throughout the free-flowing sections. The river also supports a world-class musky fishery, with trophy fish over 50 inches caught annually in the stretch between Easton and the Delaware Water Gap.

Public boat ramps are located at regular intervals along the Pennsylvania side, managed by the National Park Service in the Delaware Water Gap National Recreation Area and by state and local agencies elsewhere. The annual shad run each spring brings millions of American and hickory shad upriver, creating a beloved fishing tradition that dates back centuries. Trout inhabit the cold tributaries and upper main stem, while striped bass push upriver from the tidal Delaware during spring and fall migrations. The river's designation as a National Wild and Scenic River ensures its outstanding water quality and natural character are preserved for future generations.

name: Susquehanna River
description:
The Susquehanna River is the longest river on the East Coast and flows over 444 miles through Pennsylvania, creating one of the most extensive and productive river fisheries in the Mid-Atlantic region. The river is celebrated for its exceptional smallmouth bass fishing, with the North Branch, West Branch, and main stem all producing quality fish in the 14- to 20-inch range. Musky fishing has exploded in popularity on the Susquehanna, with trophy-class fish caught regularly in the deeper pools and around mid-river structure.

Public boat ramps are abundant along the Susquehanna's length, with the Pennsylvania Fish and Boat Commission and local municipalities maintaining dozens of access points. The stretch through Harrisburg is particularly popular and accessible, offering quality smallmouth bass and walleye fishing within sight of the state capitol. Flathead catfish, an invasive but prized sport fish, now inhabit the lower river and provide trophy fishing opportunities with fish exceeding 40 pounds. The annual shad migration brings additional excitement each spring, connecting modern anglers to a fishing tradition that predates European settlement.
